videos.transcript: This video is closed-captioned Ultimate Cancun Travel Guide: The Do’s And Don’ts Of Cancun Hello everyone, and welcome to my channel. I hope you are well and enjoying the day. Cancun this Mexican city bordering the Caribbean Sea is highly popular for tourism, especially in winters. You can visit the numerous beaches, enjoy the cenotes, visit ancient ruins, and much more. If you are visiting Cancun, you should equip yourself with some authentic local travel advice. Lucky for you I live here for the past 18 years and I will be guiding you to the top ten do's and don'ts of Cancun today. So, let’s begin. If you enjoy our videos join us on our journey around the world by subscribing to our channel please click the bell icon to get notified when we publish a new video. #1 Arriving At Cancun Airport If you are a new arrival to Cancun get ready for an Experience here, the sharks are waiting inside and outside for you, After clearing immigration and Customs you will exit through the automatic doors at Terminal 3 into another room right before the exit doors where you will find a swarm of representatives or disguised as concierge waiting in line to talk to the next tourist walking by, they are very convincing and many of them wearing name tags or t-shirts with resort or shuttle names to gain your trust. These guys will be very helpful and offer you to find a shuttle or taxi or try to carry your luggage or offer you some tours etc. their main goal is to invite you to a Timeshare Presentation and in exchange for your Time they will offer you free or discounted Tour tickets or free transportation to your hotel whatever it takes to get you signed up for the Timeshare deal. My advice here is just to stay cool and smile and don't engage in any conversation with those guys, just continue your walk to the exit doors to get outside of the terminal, and here will be the legitimate shuttle buses and hotel representatives waiting for you if you have a reservation at any resort. If Friends are picking you up you must Turn Left before the Last Automatic sliding door. At Terminal 4 You need to walk out straight to the Friends and Family exit do not turn Left where they send you to meet all shuttle buses and tour reps unless you have one waiting for you there from your hotel. #2 Don’t Take A Taxi At The Airport Many of you may like to reach your hotel by taking a taxi. However, this is a big don't for tourists visiting Cancun. You should never take a cab at the airport minimum cost is $70-80 which is outrageous, the same fare to get to the airport by a local taxi from downtown is around 200 pesos or 10 dollars you see the difference?. In fact, throughout the trip, you should avoid taking taxis. This is because the taxis in Cancun don't have a metered system. The drivers will charge you a fee through estimation. This puts travelers at a disadvantage because the drivers ask for insanely high amounts especially if you are a tourist and not a local. You are way better off with a shared shuttle bus to get into town for around $12 way cheaper than taking those expensive taxis. you can reserve your shuttle bus online and pre-book them so you don't get hit with that abusive expensive taxi fare, a quick online search reveals the best shuttle operators at Cancun airport. Most hotels in Cancun offer free transportation to their guests from and back to the airport during their stay in the city. So if you want to save your money when visiting this place, then always let your hotel know about transportation. They will send an air-conditioned van to pick you and your family. #3 Do Visit The Adventure Parks South of Cancun is Xcaret Group with 6 world-famous adventure parks that are unlike anything you’ve probably experienced before anywhere else. The first and most famous is Xcaret Park with its natural canals you can swim through, it offers also cultural shows, jungle hikes, and a relaxing stretch of beach. The tickets are a little costly, but this is a must-do in the area. Other very popular parks include Xenses an optical illusion park, Xplor with ziplines caves and ATV's and also rafting is offered here, Xel-Ha is also a must-do park with its waterslides, lazy natural rivers where you are tubing through the mangroves, and world-class snorkeling with fish feeding is right here inside the park also it has a floating bridge. You can find the rest of the parks on their website and buy a package deal for several parks it is cheaper. #4 Don’t Drink Tap Water I love many things about Cancun, However, when it comes to tap water, you should not drink it. This is a big don't is a thing that locals already know and only use bottled water, and as a tourist, you should be aware of. Various places in Cancun do claim that tap water is safe for drinking. But take my advice and avoid it. You should not trust any hotel entirely, no matter how good the service is or how many stars it got. Most of the people who drink tap water in Cancun end up having an upset stomach. And the last thing you want on your holiday trip is to get sick and be bound to your room. So always drink from sealed water bottles only. Most hotels in this city refill their guests' mini-fridges with water quite regularly. #5 Do Take Plenty Of Sunscreens This is a piece of advice that I give to our friends who plan to visit us in Cancun. It is no secret that visiting the beaches in this city is on top of everyone's list. The beaches in Cancun are amazing but do take plenty of sunscreen with you when visiting. You may think that one small tube will be enough. But take my word that you will run out of it sooner than you think. And if you think you can just buy cheap sunscreen from stores in Cancun, well, you should rethink. No matter where you go to buy sunscreen, it will be costly. The stores in the hotel zone of Cancun know that tourists require this item the most. That is why charges for sunscreen are pretty high. Of course, you can simply tackle this issue by picking up plenty of tubes from your own country. #6 Don’t Use Your Credit Cards Everywhere Gone are the days where people mainly used cash on trips. We all love to pay for goods, food, and hotels with credit and debit cards in today's time. However, in Cancun, you will have to be careful with your card here are some tips. If you go to a bar, restaurant, beach club, or anywhere else and you want to pay your bill let the waiter bring the Credit Card terminal to your table to charge you, never give away your card out of your hands for them to run off in the back of the store to charge the card go with them if you have too but never leave your card out of sight period or you dream vacation can turn fast into a nightmare. If you go to the ATM to get some cash, I advise you to do that only at Local Banks where the ATMs are inside guarded. There are 2 types of ATMs in this city privately owned ones that charge about a $20 fee for even a minor withdrawal of a few dollars and a bad exchange rate on top to give you Dollars instead of Pesos and you run the risk of getting your card skimmed here and before you know your accounts are empty back home. The other ATMs are owned by local banks giving out local currency Pesos instead of dollars like the tourist trap ATMs, and they are safe if you use them inside a bank branch or supermarket but make sure it has a Mexican bank name don't fall for the ones giving you dollars that will be an all-around bad experience just trust me on this one. #7 Do Leave Tips Leaving tips at restaurants has always been a debate for many people. But in Cancun, you shouldn't think much before tipping the guard, waiter, or any other person at your hotel. The top thing to do there is to give generous tips to the staff they do not earn much and they try their best to make you feel special and most rely on those tips. I understand you may be hesitant to give unnecessary tips but trust me, the quality of your hotel service will improve significantly when you do this simple task. By just providing a few bucks, The waiters will start to bring you extra food or special treats than usual. Most importantly, your mini-fridge will always be filled with the top beverages before the last stock finishes. So do yourself a favor by giving generous tips to people at the hotel. I guarantee it won't go unnoticed. #8 Don’t Lose Your Hotel Bracelet This is one of the most important things you shouldn't do in Cancun because it comes with a price tag. Almost every hotel in this city provides wristbands to their guests. You are expected to wear this bracelet throughout your stay and especially when you are going out to visit the city and I recommend hiding it somehow so they do not overcharge you for everything. These bracelets represent that you are the guest of a particular hotel and are to be shown when reentering the hotel. If you lose it, then the process of getting a new one will be costly, generally, they charge you for the all-inclusive package for that day and you get a new one so depending on the resort you are staying that can set you back a few hundred dollars So if you will be traveling in the city with a budget you cannot exceed, please don't lose your hotel bracelet. #9 Must-Do Explore Cancun's nightlife I encourage every Traveler to leave the resort and go explore the city the restaurants, bars, and discos Cancun hotel zone has to offer, visit the Congo Bongo Disco for an Unforgettable Night filled with fun and excellent shows, or stroll around the party center area where you find upscale classy style bars and High-end gourmet restaurants, you can also venture into downtown and visit different parks where the real local scene is with authentic Mexican food stalls like at Parque las palapas or go visit one of the many bars and discos around the city center and shopping malls in downtown Cancun. #10 Don’t Just Stay In The Hotel Our last advice of the day for you is that don’t just stay inside the hotel during your trip. Sure, you may have booked an all-inclusive resort in Cancun with exceptional buffets, bars, an infinity pool, and much more. But there is more to this city than just having cocktails at your hotel. So don’t just stay cooped up in your room. The city has many things to offer and is waiting for you to explore it. Venture into the downtown region to experience the local life. Go to the markets and haggle with the vendors to get a piece of local Mayan clothing or jewelry. When you visit downtown Cancun, only then will you realize how amazing and diverse the city is. If you are a water lover, we also suggest you go scuba diving at Musa the underwater sculpture park. Meanwhile, if you have kids, you can take them to the planetarium Ka-Yok for an interesting trip through time and space. There are many things to do in Cancun you will be glad for so don't stay in the hotel all the time. These are the top ten dos and don'ts of Cancun. So, if you are visiting this beautiful city, be sure to follow them. Once you reach there and follow these tips, We bet you will be thankful for our guidance. If you love watching travel videos and getting tips about different destinations, then please click on the subscribe button below because that is what our channel is about. And if you cannot wait for our next video, press on the bell icon to get an update as soon as we upload new content. Until then, Happy traveling.

videos.transcript: These are the things you should and shouldn't do on your next trip to Cancun and the surrounding areas. The first thing you shouldn't do is do not deal with the taxis at the Cancun airport. You'll be put into a very high-pressure sales situation and the taxis are run by the mafia, so definitely avoid that. Something you should do is to take the ADO bus from the airport. If you're staying in one of the cities or towns along the coast and not at a resort. For example, if you're staying in Playa del Carmen or in Tulum or Akumal or Bacalar, the ADO bus is going to be a great option for you to affordably get from the Cancun airport to where you're staying. And after you go through customs in Cancun and you get your luggage, you're going to walk out there and you're going to see the ADO bus booth and there will probably be a line of people waiting to get tickets because it's one of the best transportation options from the Cancun airport. If you're searching online looking for a resort to stay at on your next trip, don't be fooled when the resort says the resort name and then Cancun or the resort name and Tulum because often times it is actually not in Cancun or in Tulum, but reasonably close. For example, I'm staying at the Waldorf Astoria Cancun, but it's actually between Cancun and Puerto Morelos. And another example is the Conrad Tulum, which in reality is located in Akumal. And if you're staying at a place like where I'm staying and it's not all-inclusive, seriously consider renting a car, especially if the resort is isolated. Like if you're staying in a place that's not all-inclusive, but like you're walking distance from everything in Playa del Carmen, no problem if you don't have a car. You're going to be able to walk to pretty much wherever you want to go or take a a short taxi ride. However, like this resort, it's very isolated. There's no stores nearby or anything like that. And all the stuff inside the resort is pretty expensive. So, having a car gives us a lot of freedom for our vacation here. And it also helps us save a bunch of money by going out to Puerto Morelos for a lot of our meals. The next don't I have for you is if you're renting a car, do not rent from Hertz. Okay, so I booked my ticket on Aeromexico's website, my plane ticket to get here. And then after I purchased that, they offered me a car rental from Hertz and it was a pretty good price or decent price at least. So, I'm like, yeah, sure. I think it'd be convenient to have a car while there. So, I went ahead and purchased that. Well, then I got here to Hertz and it turns out they're running a giant scam operation. And when I went to reveal their scam to you guys, they tried to put me in jail. So, it's a total nightmare. Do not rent from Hertz and be very careful about renting from the other major car rental companies here as well. But, down in the description of this video, I'm going to link to a small car rental company that's honest and reliable and isn't running a scam on you. So, be sure to check that out if you want to rent a car in Cancun. The next thing you should do is go explore outside of your resort. Like, especially if you have a rental car. But, you can also hire a cab for the day or something like that. You might not realize it, but there's a long history of Mayan civilizations here and there's ancient ruins around. For example, I highly recommend the Tulum archaeological site and the Coba ruins as well. Both of those are a really cool experience. The next thing you shouldn't do is don't believe the lies the salespeople will tell you at the Cancun Airport. For example, one of these big lies is they'll they'll a fake price sheet laminated in their hands and they'll be trying to sell you a taxi to wherever you're going. And if you want to compare the prices of like an ADO bus for example to your destination versus a taxi, well, they'll have highly elevated prices on their fake sheet to show you, well, here's the cost of the ADO, here's the cost of a taxi and it will make the taxi cost look reasonable. And another lie they'll tell you, they'll ask you if you have transportation yet and if you say yes, they'll ask you, oh, who are you going with? And then you say the name of the company whether it's some shuttle company or the ADO bus and they'll say, oh, they're no longer in business or oh, the ADO bus actually isn't running right now. But these lies are very common because they're just trying to steal a sale from that other company and sell you an overpriced taxi. As a matter of fact, this just happened to one of my friends who lives here. Uh, he lives uh, right down the road in Puerto Morelos and just to see, he went up to one of the taxi guys and he's like, how much to Puerto Morelos? And they quoted him like 1,400 pesos or or some ridiculous number for the short drive it is to Puerto Morelos and he goes, uh, no, I think I'll just take the ADO. And the guy goes, oh, actually the ADO is closed right now. And he's like, oh, really? And then he actually went to the ADO booth and asked them if they're still running and they're like, yes, we're going to Puerto Morelos every 15 minutes. The next thing you should do is if you're staying at one of the hotels that's operated by a major hotel company here, be sure to check out what credit card options are available from that brand. So, think like if you're staying at a Hilton or a Marriott. For example, I'm staying at the Waldorf Astoria Cancun, which is part of the Hilton chain and I I a Hilton credit card that gets me automatic top tier status in Hilton, which saved me like $500 on food here. And also, it got me about a $10,000 room upgrade during my stay, which was incredible, just by having a simple credit card. So, I know there's a credit card for Hilton that does this, and I know there's one for Marriott that does this. So, be sure to check that out. The next thing you should not do is forget your sunscreen, because a lot of times the lobster people outnumber the regular people here. Uh and if you don't bring sunscreen and you're going to a resort, it's going to be very expensive to buy it at a resort. It will probably be about $30, but you can find affordable sunscreen here at grocery stores, pharmacies, and a lot of times at convenience stores as well. A lot of Americans and Canadians love coming to Mexico and visiting one of these beautiful resorts. And I'm not knocking them. It is an awesome experience. But the next thing you should do is consider spending some time outside of a resort. For example, if you stay at a hotel in Playa del Carmen that isn't a resort, you're going to save a ton of money. You're probably still going to have an awesome time. And with all the money you saved, you'll be able to come back sooner. The next thing you should not do, this is a really important one, is do not exceed the duty-free limit for alcohol or tobacco products. I've gotten so many comments on this channel of people who said they came into the Cancun airport exceeding the number of packs of cigarettes they were allowed to bring into the country and got totally screwed with massive fines. I remember one lady commenting saying she got hit with a $1,500 fine for exceeding the number of cigarette packs she was allowed to bring in. At that time, the limit was 10 packs of cigarettes per person, but they might have increased that. And if they have, I'll put the adjusted number on the screen right here. So, let's say you're coming to the area for a week. I think one thing you should definitely do is take at least one day trip during your time here. So, one thing you could do is like taking a catamaran tour to Isla Mujeres. So, you get on this catamaran. A lot of times it's in Cancun and then you'll sail around where it's unlimited booze while you're out there and then you'll arrive at Isla Mujeres. You might have a couple hours to spend exploring Playa Norte and the shops there. It's a really cool experience and so much fun. Another recommendation I have for you is any of the Xcaret experiences. There's a reason Xcaret experiences are so popular and it is because they are really great at making sure you have an awesome time. The next thing you should do is take into account the time of year you want to visit. The best weather of the year is going to be December to February and the worst weather is probably August and September. However, pricing can be drastically different between the various months of the year. So, going out to restaurants, the pricing is probably going to be the same no matter what time of year. However, the price of the hotels can vary so much. For example, I've seen a hotel on the beach in Tulum that's $100 a night during the low season and $700 a night during the most popular months. And the next thing you shouldn't do is if you're coming during one of the hotter months of the year, do not forget that staying beachfront makes all the difference in the world in terms of how comfortable you're going to be on your stay. Like, yeah, if you come in August or September, it's going to be super hot and really humid. And if you're not beachfront, it will be very uncomfortable weather. However, if you're beachfront, at least for me, it is still very enjoyable because that ocean breeze makes all the difference in the world. A lot of people think Mexico is just beaches and desert. And the next thing I think you should do is do come and enjoy Mexico's beautiful beaches, but at the same time, realize that's not all that Mexico is. I did live here in this area for a couple of years, but right now I'm living in central Mexico, and where I live, it's at higher elevation, the weather is beautiful all year round, and the city is about 500 years old, so it has this gorgeous colonial Spanish architecture all over the center of the city there. All I'm trying to say is there's a lot of other really awesome vacation destinations in Mexico that aren't at the beach. So, if you come to Mexico and you see a price tag, don't get confused and think that it's in dollars because the same symbol we use for a dollar in the US or in Canada, they use for the Mexican peso here. So, all prices are going to be in Mexican pesos unless it's clearly marked otherwise, and it will say x amount of dollars or USD or something like that. And so you can easily do the conversion. For about the past 5 years, ever since I've been living in Mexico, the exchange rate has hovered around 20 pesos to $1. It's gotten as high as 25, as low as 17, but it's always been relatively close to 20 to 1. And for you to easily do a conversion from pesos to dollars, drop a zero, then divide by two. For example, if it's 400 pesos, get rid of one zero, it turns into 40, divide by two, it's 20. So, 400 pesos is $20. And do realize that all prices in Mexico already include a 16% sales tax. So, if you ever see sales tax added on to something you're buying, added on to the price that was on the menu or something like that, there's a good chance that they're trying to weasel a little bit of extra money out of you. The next thing you should do is if you're going to a resort, arrange your airport transportation ahead of time, whether that's renting a cab or communicating with the hotel you're staying at and having them send a car for you. In fact, a lot of hotels and resorts include transportation to and from the airport. Or maybe this means booking private transportation. Booking private transportation is going to be so much better for you than taking a cab because you're not going to be put into a high-pressure sales situation with the people trying to sell you taxis, and you're going to have someone standing there with your name on it waiting for you after you walk out of the doors of the Cancun Airport. And in addition to taking you to your hotel, they'll also stop by the grocery store for you or stop by convenience store so you can get some snacks and some liquor, maybe some other mixers for your trip, and it's going to make your trip a whole lot better. When we arrived in the Cancun Airport, my said to me, "What a nightmare. This is worse than arriving in Vegas." And it is a nightmare. There's so many people, it's so hectic, there's so many people trying to sell you things and lie to you and scam you. Just trust me, you want to avoid it all and book that transportation ahead of time. I'll link to some transportation options down in the description below. The next thing you shouldn't do is don't let cops bully you if you're driving. This unfortunately is one of the parts of the country where it's more common to have police try to shake you down, but something you can do is there is a law here that states that tourists here in Quintana Roo must be given a warning for minor infractions. So, I'm going to link to something down in the description that you can print out a couple copies of before your trip. And if you ever get pulled over, just give that to the cop. It will say the name of the law and say in Spanish, and that is going to help you avoid a lot of the shakedowns. But in addition to that, something the cops look for here is people driving their rental car back to the Cancun airport. They specifically look for rental cars on the airport road, and a lot of the rental cars are marked so they'll know who's driving a rental car because like you're going to the airport, you need to return your rental car pretty quickly, otherwise you could miss your flight. So, they know that this is a perfect time for a shakedown, and that's why I think you should go down in the description of this video and look for the guy I recommend who rents cars because his cars don't have anything that indicate it's a rental car. The next thing you shouldn't do is don't forget to bring a credit card that has zero foreign transaction fees because when you're here in Mexico, paying with a credit card is going to be your best option for getting hit with the least amount of foreign exchange fees. So, if you like bring dollars and you go to a money exchanger, you're probably going to lose at 5 to 10% each way converting into pesos and then back into dollars. Or if you bring a bunch of dollars and are paying with dollars everywhere, well then you're probably going to be taking about a 10% hit most of the time because a lot of businesses really hit you on that exchange rate because they're going to have to take your dollars and convert them back into pesos, which is going to cost them money. Uh granted, there are some businesses that don't keep up day-to-day with the exchange rate fluctuations, and sometimes it actually is better to pay in dollars, but that's the exception, not the rule. But, having a credit card that has no foreign transaction fees is going to give you great exchange rates and allow your money to go a lot further when you're here on vacation. And if it charges foreign transaction fees, you're going to be charged an additional 3% on every single purchase. So, look for the travel-branded credit cards. Almost every single credit card that's branded as a travel card, whether that's through an airline or a hotel, it's probably not going to have foreign transaction fees, but you can always double-check. Oh, and in terms of credit card, Visa and MasterCard are accepted pretty much everywhere, and American Express is accepted in like 90% of places around here. However, there are some places that will say, "Hey, if you want to pay with a credit card, it's going to be 3%, 4%, 5% extra." That's fairly common, especially in some small towns. For example, going to Isla Holbox, that was super common there. Almost every place that did accept credit cards charged a fee for doing so. But, if you're in one of these most popular tourist destinations, so Cancun, Playa del Carmen, Puerto Morelos, Tulum, Akumal, almost all the places here are going to accept credit cards, and most places do not charge an extra fee for doing so. videos.description:
Top 5 Things to Do Around Cancun Mexico shows you great excursions in the Yucatan Peninsula around Cancun, Playa Del Carmen, Tulum, and Merida. If you're looking for more than just beaches and resorts make sure to check out these top 5 Things to do around Cancun. First we start out in Chichen Itza, one of the seven wonders of the world. Next we head to Valladolid which is a great base to get a hotel and explore the surrounding areas. From here you can visit Chichen Itza, other ruins, and plenty of Cenotes. Our two favorite Cenotes being Cenote Suytun and Cenote Xcanahaltun. If you're up for a drive then head to the amazing town of Bacalar which we would argue is more beautiful than Cancun, Tulum, or Playa Del Carmen with out the touristy vibes. Here you can rent a boat on Lake Bacalar and travel to Los Rapidos. Last but not least we drove to the Archaeological Zone of Becán where we saw the most amazing Mayan Ruins. There are plenty of ruins closer to Cancun but we loved that you could climb up the pyramids here. Becan was completely empty and truly amazing to see!

videos.transcript: One of the things that you must do while in the Cancun area is visit some of the breathtaking watering holes they call cenotes. The Yucatan Peninsula doesn't have any lakes or above ground rivers, the population actually gets their freshwater from a series of underground rivers and more than 6000 cenotes. These cenotes can be visible from surface or they can be deep into caves. Some are shallow and others are hundreds of feet deep. Oh, that's freaking high. Okay. That's one way to enter. Just be prepared, the water in them is quite cold, but after the first 30 seconds or so, the temperature is actually pretty comfortable. How do you get to them? Well, you can drive to many of them on your own if you're renting a car and you can even take a cheap bus or colectivo to some of them, but you'll probably be interested in some of the other things that I suggest in this video and many of the tours that you can take around Cancun include a stop at a cenote. So that may be a way to check out some cenotes without increasing your trip expenditures at all. The next stop is Playa Caracol. This small but very pretty beach is located on the northern end of the hotel zone, just a short walk from the Cancun nightclub scene and right across the street from the Hotel Aloft where I stayed on my recent trip to Cancun. If you're not staying nearby, you can get here from almost anywhere in Cancun by taking the r-1 or r-2 bus line, which costs less than a dollar each way. The Cancun bus system is very easy to use, but you can open Google Maps to help you navigate them. This bus stops right at the entrance to the beach, which is really convenient. The public beach is free to enter and if you want a very affordable beach day, you can walk across the street to the Chedraui Selecto, a fancy grocery store, and buy any food or drink that you want for the day. There are also many restaurants within walking distance of Playa Caracol as well. If you want it, and if you get there early enough to snag one, you can also rent chairs and an umbrella for not too much money. To get away from the hotel for the day and enjoy the beautiful nature of Cancun. One of my favorite things to do is an Isla Mujeres catamaran tour. On this tour, you'll spend a few hours cruising around the calm, beautiful waters of Cancun and Isla Mujeres. The tour will vary a little bit depending on which one you book, but it will likely include a lunch at Isla Mujeres, and then they'll give you a couple hours to explore the island. You might stop for a swim at the beautiful Playa Norte or go snorkeling at the Cancun Underwater Museum. These tours typically include unlimited booze as well. Unless you're paying the big bucks get a private catamaran, you're probably going to be sharing it with other people. So here's a pro tip for you. When everyone boards the boat, immediately snag any seat in the shade by putting your belongings on that seat. You'll be free to roam around the boat and spend as much time in the sun as you want, but I guarantee that you'll want to relax in that shaded spot from time to time. The next thing I highly recommend that you do while in Cancun is go to Xcaret. This is an almost indescribable experience. It's an ecological park, it's a water park, it's an amusement park, and it's an archaeological site, all in one. There are so many things to do at Xcaret that you could spend several days there without ever repeating a single experience. There are more than 50 different activities in the park, so there are far too many to cover in this video you could go swimming in one of the underground rivers or see the wildlife such as the flamingos or the sea turtles. In addition to these things, you can also go see the on-site ancient ruins attend one of their incredible cultural experiences or visit their representation of a typical Mexican cemetery. And while that might sound kind of dark or creepy, it's actually really colorful and a pretty experience. You can get substantial discounts to Xcaret if you buy your tickets at least 3 weeks in advance on their website. If you buy tickets to multiple parks, or if you purchased them from a Costco in Mexico. If you want to party while in Cancun, check out one of the popular night clubs. One recommendation I have for you Coco Bongo because it's more than just a night club. It isn't cheap but they put on an awesome show for several hours and it's really great experience. If you decide to go to Coco Bongo, I'd recommend giving the hostess a great tip of about 20 dollars when you arrive to help ensure that you get great service and one of the best in the house. I suggest this because the biggest complaint that people have is that they didn't get good seats. By the way, you can usually get discounted Coco Bongo tickets at any Costco in Mexico or on the Costco Mexico website. If you want a more typical nightclub experience, I recommend Monkey Business because they have great live music, a fun party atmosphere, and it's a bit cheaper than the other nearby night clubs. Located at a bit north of Tulum, Xel-ha is one of the incredible experiences that Xcaret offers in the area. Xel-ha might be described as Xcaret's water park cousin. Here you're gonna find many attractions mostly themed around water. There are gorgeous cenotes, huge water slides, ziplines that drop you into the water, small rivers that you can go tubing on, and amazing turquoise water from the Caribbean. You can also take a bike ride through the jungle paths or go up to the top of the lighthouse tower to get incredible views of the area. And if you don't know how to swim, no worry there because provide life vests for every activity. It's worth noting that the cenotes at Xel-ha are protected so you can't swim in them, but despite that there are still lots things to experience here. If you're interested in checking out Xel-ha, you can get discounted tickets by purchasing them in advance on their website or by buying them from Costco Mexico. Another cool thing to do is going snorkeling or scuba diving at the reef. Just offshore from Cancun is the Mesoamerican barrier reef, The second largest barrier reef in the world. It runs for 700 miles along the coast of Mexico, Belize, and even further south from there. So if you want to see the world under the water, you can take a snorkeling or scuba diving tour to the reef. Something else you can do for almost free in Cancun is to head to the gorgeous public beach of Playa Delfines, where you can enjoy the beautiful turquoise waters and white sand. To get here from the hotel zone, just make sure that you're on the correct side of street so that your bus is going in the right direction and flag down any r-1 or r-2 bus that you see. These buses will only drop off at a bus stop, but they'll typically pick up from anywhere if you just reach your hand out as if you're flagging a taxi. The bus will cost less than a dollar per person. Once you arrive at the beach, it's free to enter and there will probably be vendors selling food and drinks. However, there are no stores or restaurants nearby. So be sure to bring any food or refreshments that you might want during the day. If you arrive to Playa Delfines early enough in the day, you can snag a free chair and umbrella, but if you arrive later and there isn't any shade available, you can always throw down your own towel anywhere. While you're here, be sure to take a picture with the Cancun sign as a little something remember your vacation. If you want a more upscale beach experience, head to one of the many beach clubs where you can rent a nice shaded beachfront lounger for the day while you're served food and cocktails. One example of this is Zama Beach Club on Isla Mujeres, but if you want to remain in the hotel zone, Mandala Beach club might be a good option for you. The way that most these beach clubs work is the chair and umbrella are free. You just have to buy enough food and drinks to meet the minimum consumption requirement, which might 50 dollars a person, give or take, depending on which club you go to. If you want to see some ancient ruins without leaving the hotel zone, you can head to the El Rey Archaeological Site. Depending on where you're staying, it might actually be within walking distance of your hotel. But if it's not, you can easily hop on one of the r-1 or r-2 buses heading in that direction to there easily and affordably. It only costs a few dollars to enter and it's typically not busy at all. You can even get a private tour with a professional guide for about 10 dollars during which they'll teach you a lot about the history of the area. La Isla Mall in Cancun is a vibrant and popular shopping destination located in the heart of the hotel zone, it offers a unique open air shopping experience with a stunning waterfront location. The mall features over a hundred and 50 stores including fancy stores like Prada and others I can't pronounce, but you also have local boutiques and cheaper options like H&M, catering to a diverse range of tastes and budgets. Visitors can enjoy strolling along the picturesque canals that run through the mall which are reminiscent of Venice, Italy. In addition to shopping, La Isla Mall offer a variety of dining options from casual eateries to upscale restaurants serving both local and international cuisine. If you want to party while getting a glimpse into one of Mexico's vibrant traditions, check out Xochimilco by Xcaret. Inspired by the famous Mexico City canals, Xochimilco offers a super fun atmosphere where you'll float around on the canals for a few hours in a colorful flat bottom boat while enjoying unlimited booze, live music, and Mexican food. One reviewer on Google describes this experience as the most fun they've ever had in their life. You can get discounted tickets on the Xcaret website by bundling with other Xcaret experiences or by purchasing your tickets in advance. If you want to change a scenery from Cancun, take the short ferry ride over to Isla Mujeres. Isla Mujeres, or the island of women as it translates to English, has some of the most beautiful beaches I've ever seen in my life. While at the island, you can enjoy the gorgeous scenery while sipping on a cocktail from one of the many beach bars. You can explore a small part of the island on foot, but if you'd like to have a bit of fun and see more of the island, you can rent a golf cart for a few hours. There are multiple ferry options to get over to Isla Mujeres, but if you choose the Xcaret option, you get to go up to the top of the Cancun Scenic Tower totally free. This tower experience lasts about 15 minutes, taking you 260 feet into the air as the capsule slowly spins all the way around giving you 360 degree views of Cancun and the surrounding area. The views from the top are absolutely stunning. The ticket for just the tower experience goes for like 20 dollars a person, but you can buy an Isla Mujeres ferry ticket on the Xcaret website for just 13 dollars, and that price includes a free scenic tower ticket. It's tough to beat that value. You might notice that I'm mentioning Xcaret a lot in this video. This video isn't sponsored, it's just that Xcaret really knows what they're doing and they know how to create awesome experiences. As a matter of fact, there are many other Xcaret experiences in the area that I intentionally left out of this video, not because they're not awesome, but just because I didn't want half the video to be about Xcaret Next, you can visit the Mayan Museum and the archaeological site of San Miguelito. Located in the Cancun hotel zone, it offers visitors a unique opportunity to explore the rich history and heritage of the ancient Maya civilization. Adjacent to the museum, the San Miguelito archaeological zone, features the remains of a Mayan settlement dating back to the 1200s where you can explore the restored structures, including temples, plazas, and residential areas, gaining a deeper understanding of the architectural prowess and urban planning of the ancient Maya. To get there, if staying in the hotel zone, just hop on one of the r-1 or r-2 buses heading in that direction and then get off at the nearest bus stop. It'll cost you less than dollar. By the way, I've mentioned these buses a few times in this video. The Cancun bus system is super easy to use and affordable. If you want to get to the cancun hotel zone from the airport without spending an arm and a leg on a taxi, you can buy a cheap ADO bus ticket to Cancun Centro, and from there, take the r-1 or r-2 bus into the hotel zone. Marina Puerto Cancun is another great shopping mall that you can check out. It's located right next to the Marina, so you have views of the water from the mall. And in addition to shopping, you can visit one of its many restaurants or go grab an ice cream and take a stroll along the boardwalk next to the water. Puerto Maya Cancun is one of the highest rated tours in the Cancun Hotel zone. For 69 dollars a person, you get to cruise through the jungle in a little speedboat, go snorkeling, have lunch, and see a Mayan show. When talking about bang for the buck, this is one of the better value tours in the area. If you want to get away from Cancun for a day or two, one great option go check out the magic town of Valladolid Located about a 2 and a half hour drive inland, Valladolid, as we know it today, dates back to the colonial era however, the mayans were living here long before then. While in Valladolid, I recommend getting a Marquesita in the center square. You can choose your toppings, but my personal favorite combination is Nutella and Banana. While there, have a couple meals at various restaurants because Valladolid has some of my favorite food that I've tried in all of Mexico. One restaurant I recommend you check out is Ix Cat Ik Mayan cuisine. If you have a few extra days to spare, I highly recommend that you go check out Bacalar. Located about 4 hours drive south of Cancun, you can arrive here by bus or car. Bacalar, the town, sits adjacent to the Bacalar lagoon, which is also known as the lagoon of 7 colors. This is one of the prettiest places that I've been to all across Mexico. While there, I recommend trying some of the awesome food and taking a kayak out onto the water. Here you can see me and my dog, Laska having time of our lives out on the lagoon. Located about 30 minutes south of Cancun is the little town of Puerto Morelos. I was actually living here for a couple of years. It's a quaint little beach town with a nice vibe and you'll find that Puerto Morelos is quite a bit cheaper than the Cancun hotel zone. While in Puerto Morelos, you should go take a picture with iconic leaning lighthouse that was blown over by a hurricane in the 1960s. And I also recommend that you enjoy some of the great restaurants that the town has to offer such as Boquinete. Additionally, if you want a nice beach club experience without spending a ton of money, you can go check out Unico Beach Club. If you're planning to stay overnight in Puerto Morelos, you should know that there are 2 parts of the town. There's the part of the town by the beach where you'll probably want to stay, and then a couple kilometers away is the other side of town or what they call La Colonia. So when you're booking, If you want to be by the beach, just make sure your hotel or your Airbnb is definitely on the beach side. Another awesome place that you can visit on your trip cancun is the Tulum archaeological site. Located about 90 minutes south of the Cancun airport, I think the Tulum ruins are definitely worth a visit. What makes this archeological site amazing for me is its location right next to the gorgeous Caribbean Sea. As of this video, parking at ruins cost a bit less than 10 dollars, and the entrance fee is about 5 dollars per person, but entry is free on Sundays. You can find tours that will take you to the Tulum ruins, the Coba ruins, and incredible cenotes, all in one day. For an indescribably awesome experience while in Cancun, you can go swim with the sea turtles in crystal clear turquoise water. For the best experience, you're probably going to want to head to Akumal. Akumal is located about halfway between Playa Del Carmen and Tulum. The easiest but most expensive way to swim with the sea turtles would be to book an all inclusive tour that includes transportation from your hotel, equipment, and a guide. Whereas the most economical option would be to take public transportation to Akumal, then rent snorkel equipment from the Akumal dive shop and go snorkeling without a guide. So, based on your budget, there's different options for you. Next is Chichen Itza. This is one of the 7 wonders of the modern world, The focal point of Chichen Itza, the one that you always see is the grand pyramid known as El Castillo or the Temple of Kukulcan. This architectural marvel is a representation of the Mayan calendar with 91 steps on each of its 4 sides, adding up to 365 steps in total. The site also boasts other remarkable structures such as the Temple of the Warriors, a massive stone complex adorned with intricate carvings and columns, the great ball court stands out as the largest of its kind in Mesoamerica, featuring stone rings high on the walls where a ball game was played, demonstrating the Mayan's athletic prowess. Chichen Itza's historical significance is evident in its well preserved structures and the cultural legacy it represents. The site attracts countless visitors who are captivated by its rich history, architectural marvels, and the profound insights it provides into the ancient mayan civilization. It's a testament to human ingenuity and the wonders of the past. If you want to visit Chichen Itza on your trip to Cancun, plan on it taking an entire day. There are many Chichen Itza tours that you can book, and I'd recommend choosing one that also stops in the magic town of Valladolid. It's a neat little town with incredible that also gives you a glimpse into what colonial Mexico is like. Coba is another really neat archaeological site that you can visit. It's an ancient Mayan city located about 45 minutes from Tulum. Once home to more than 50000 people, here you'll find one of tallest mayan structures in the region. Surrounded by lush jungle, Coba offers a unique and adventurous experience as you can rent bicycles or hire guides to explore this expansive archaeological site and discover all of the hidden temples. When I last visited a few years ago, you were able to climb the tallest pyramid, but I've recently heard that it isn't being allowed. If in the future they allow visitors to climb it again, I highly recommend it because it's really awesome to have that view from above the jungle canopy. I thought that visiting Coba was a really fun experience because you bike through these jungle paths as you encounter many different ancient ruins, and the breeze from riding around on a bike helps to keep you cool on those hot days. To book tours to any of these places that I mentioned, you could buy from the tour salesperson in your hotel or from a tour booth on the street. But if you buy from one of those places, there's no way to know the quality of the tour you're booking beforehand. So to find the very best excursions and tours, I recommend you do one of 3 things. Number 1, you can Google, "Cancun Experiences Airbnb" and look at the highest rated experiences on Airbnb. The closer to 5 stars, the better. For example, if an Airbnb experience is rated 4.95 and it has a hundred reviews, you know it's going to be worth it. My second recommendation is that you Google "best tours Cancun TripAdvisor", and you'll see the user ratings of all the most popular tours in the area on TripAdvisor. Don't hesitate to book any that are 5 stars. Or the third great option is you can go into the description of this video, and I'll link to some awesome tours for you. Next, watch this video on what not to do in Cancun.

videos.title: TOP 17 Things To Do In Cancun 🇲🇽 Travel Guide
videos.description:
Cancun, located on the northeastern coast of the Yucatán Peninsula in Mexico, is a premier travel destination known for its stunning beaches, vibrant nightlife, and rich cultural heritage.
🎟 DISCOUNTED Tours & Tickets for Cancun ➡ https://getyourguide.tp.st/DuvnpkcK

0:00 Intro
0:25 Snorkeling
0:42 Resort Life
0:59 Nightlife at Hotel Zone
1:15 Chichen Itza
1:32 Interactive Aquarium Cancun
1:49 Day at the Beach
2:07 Parasailing
2:24 El Rey Archaeological Zone
2:42 MUSA (Cancun Underwater Museum)
3:02 La Isla
3:19 Xoximilco Park
3:37 Punta Sur, Isla Mujeres
3:56 Mercado 28
4:15 Captain Hook Cancun
4:33 Ventura Park
4:51 Mayan Museum of Cancun
5:10 Authentic Mexican Cuisine

Cancun Mexico offers a plethora of activities and attractions that cater to all types of travelers. Whether you're seeking adventure, relaxation, or cultural exploration, there are countless things to do in Cancun that will make your trip unforgettable.

One of the top things to do in Cancun is visiting the ancient Mayan ruins. Sites like Chichén Itzá, Tulum, and El Rey provide a glimpse into the rich history of the region. These archaeological wonders are among the most popular Cancun tourist attractions and are must-visits for history enthusiasts. For those wondering what to do in Cancun beyond historical sites, exploring cenotes—natural sinkholes filled with crystal-clear water—is a unique experience that combines adventure and natural beauty. Swimming or snorkeling in these cenotes is undoubtedly one of the coolest things to do in Cancun.

Beach lovers will find paradise along Cancun's pristine coastline. Playa Delfines and Playa Tortugas are two iconic spots where you can relax on golden sands or partake in water sports like snorkeling and jet skiing. Cancun sightseeing isn’t complete without a visit to Isla Mujeres, a nearby island accessible by ferry. This serene destination offers picturesque beaches, vibrant coral reefs, and opportunities for snorkeling and diving at the famous MUSA Underwater Museum.

For those seeking fun things to do in Cancun Mexico after sunset, the nightlife scene is unparalleled. Coco Bongo, a world-renowned nightclub, offers an electrifying mix of music, acrobatics, and entertainment. Meanwhile, Parque de las Palapas in downtown Cancun provides a more laid-back atmosphere with local food vendors and cultural events.

Cancun activities also include eco-adventures like visiting Xcaret Park or Xplor Adventure Park. These parks offer zip-lining, underground river tours, and encounters with exotic wildlife. Shopping enthusiasts can explore La Isla Shopping Village for high-end boutiques or local markets for authentic souvenirs.

A Cancun travel guide wouldn't be complete without mentioning the Museo Maya de Cancún. This museum showcases Mayan artifacts and provides insight into pre-Hispanic culture. Coupled with nearby ruins like San Miguelito, it’s one of the best things to do in Cancun for history buffs.

Whether you’re planning your first visit to Cancun or returning for another adventure, this tropical paradise offers endless opportunities for exploration and relaxation. From breathtaking beaches to cultural landmarks and thrilling adventures, there’s no shortage of Cancun attractions to discover.

The things to do in Cancun range from pristine beaches and ancient ruins to vibrant nightlife and thrilling adventures. Whether you're seeking relaxation or excitement, things to do in Cancun Mexico provide something for every type of traveler.

Among the best things to do in Cancun are exploring the stunning beaches that line the Hotel Zone. Playa Delfines offers pristine white sand and crystal-clear turquoise waters, while Playa Tortugas provides a more lively atmosphere with beachfront restaurants and entertainment. These coastal gems represent some of the most fun things to do in Cancun for beach enthusiasts and sun worshippers alike.

The top things to do in Cancun extend far beyond the shoreline. Adventure seekers can explore mysterious cenotes, natural sinkholes filled with fresh water that offer incredible swimming and snorkeling opportunities. These underground caves provide some of the most unique things to do in Cancun, allowing visitors to swim in crystal-clear pools surrounded by stunning rock formations.

For those wondering what to do in Cancun beyond beaches and cenotes, the region's rich Mayan heritage offers fascinating cultural experiences. Day trips to Chichen Itza, one of the New Seven Wonders of the World, showcase ancient pyramids and temples. Closer to the city, the El Rey Archaeological Zone and the Mayan Museum provide glimpse into pre-Columbian civilization, making them essential places to visit in Cancun for history buffs.

When the sun sets, things to do in Cancun at night come alive with world-class entertainment. The famous Coco Bongo nightclub features spectacular acrobatic shows and tribute performances, while downtown Cancun's Parque Las Palapas offers authentic local culture with food vendors and live music. These venues represent some of the cool things to do in Cancun after dark.

Adventure parks like Xcaret and Xplor provide family-friendly excitement with zip-lining, underground river tours, and cultural shows. Meanwhile, boat trips to nearby Isla Mujeres offer pristine beaches and the famous MUSA underwater museum, where visitors can snorkel among submerged sculptures.

Marine life enthusiasts can swim with whale sharks during summer months or encounter sea turtles at Akumal Bay. The Nichupte Lagoon provides kayaking opportunities through mangrove channels, while sport fishing excursions offer chances to catch marlin and sailfish.

The things to see in Cancun include vibrant coral reefs perfect for diving and snorkeling, luxury shopping at La Isla Village, and the scenic Cancun Tower offering panoramic views. These diverse Cancun tourist attractions ensure that every visitor finds their perfect adventure.

From cultural immersion to adrenaline-pumping activities, what to see in Cancun encompasses natural wonders, ancient civilizations, and modern entertainment. This comprehensive mix of experiences makes Cancun travel unforgettable, whether you're planning a romantic getaway, family vacation, or adventure-filled expedition. The endless variety of Cancun things to do guarantees that things to do Cancun will exceed every expectation, making Mexico Cancun a premier destination that beckons travelers to return again and again.

videos.transcript: Cancun, a Mexican city on the Yucatan Peninsula bordering the Caribbean Sea, is known for its white sandy beaches, stunning scenery, and cultural experiences. It's composed of two distinct areas, the more traditional downtown area and a long beachfront strip of high-rise hotels, shops, and restaurants. Cancun is a popular destination among Mexico's Mayan Riviera, where there's so much to do and experience. Before we get into the top 10 things to do in Cancun, we've included links in the description to various discount codes and links to resources of things to do. So, make sure you check those out. At number 10 is Mayan ruins of Tulum. Tulum was built in the 13th century and it's one of the best surviving relics of the Mayan post-classic period. Perched on the edge of a 12 m limestone cliff, visitors can wander through the centuries old temples, marvel at the ancient fresco wall paintings, and get a sense of what life was like in Mayan Mexico. Number nine is Parky Maya Cancun. Embark on a theatrical journey into Mexico's Mayan history at Parky Maya Cancun. The adventure into the past begins with a short film tribute to Mayan culture, giving visitors a fascinating insight into one of history's most interesting civilizations and preparing you for the adventures to come. Fly across zip lines and ropes high above the Nishupe lagoon and cruise along the mangraves below. Then trek across hanging rope bridges and dense jungle to ancient temple ruins where the Maya made their famous calendar. At number eight is Playa Delfine. In a quiet corner of the hotel zone south of the Musea Maya, Playa Delfine is a blue flag beach where you can find peace away from Cancun's crowds. Long and wide, it is a public beach with coarse white sand and powerful waves. Padeline has a designated swimming area watched by a lifeguard, and you can always paddle in the water without having to worry. There's also a beach bar for snacks and drinks, and you can use the palm shelters for free. Number seven is Rio Secredo. See a secret underground cave close to Playa del Carman and explore amazing geological formations. Guests can enjoy a fact-filled underground walk with your guide and swim in crystal clearar waters. The Rio Secredo glistens with natural wonders and visitors will be mesmerized by millions of years of history deep down under the ground. At number six is Explore Park. Enjoy action-packed activities at Explore Park and zoom down one of Latin America's tallest ziplining courses with 14 different lines. Jump on board an amphibious vehicle to drive through jungle trails, over suspended bridges, and even below ground into mysterious caverns to the underground river. Journey deep below the surface to discover ancient underground rivers which you can swim through or explore by raft. It's all the fun of the jungle and a Mexican must do for visitors. Number five is Elray ruins. Located in the heart of the hotel zone, these ruins are extremely accessible to visitors and the site's small size makes it easy to see in a short time. Once a center for maritime trade, Elray dates back to 1200 AD and you'll get great views of the lagoon as you explore what life was like for ancient mine communities. At number four is the underwater museum and visitor center. There are few art installations anywhere in the world quite like the Cancun underwater museum. The museum comprises of over 500 statues completely submerged in the Cancun National Marine Park waters. The best way to see these alternative coral reefs in familiar shapes is by snorkeling up close. But if you don't want to get wet, you can also see them via a glass-bottomed boat. The visitor center serves as an art gallery with pictures and information about the project. Number three is Delphinus Chelha. Visitors can hop into the pool with some very friendly and playful dolphins, as well as other unforgettable interactive activities. With many attractions, including water slides, snorkeling with 90 species of exotic sea life, jungle tres, cave pools, and much more. This eco-friendly resort paradise is a great day out for all the family. At number two, marvel at the ruins of ancient Chitanita. The ancient city of Chitanita is a large Mayan archaeological site and one of the most notable and recognized landmarks. The site itself was originally a main hub of Mayan civilization and has since been named a UNESCO World Heritage site. The focal point of the ruins is the pyramid-like ElCastilo that was once used by the Mayans as a temple to the god Kukulkan. And at number one is Eshkarat. Discover a world of eco adventure in the leafy island paradise of Eshkarat. Developed around the legendary archaeological site, Eshkarat is overflowing with family-friendly activities and thrilling cultural experiences. Visitors can sail the Paradise River, trekk to the Mayan village, explore Jaguar Island, and much more. With dozens of live performances, adrenaline charged rides, and traditional experiences, it's the perfect adventurous escape. So, that sums up our top 10 things to do in Cancun. We hope you enjoyed.
